questions.text: I notice when someone volunteers to leave a room to give others privacy they did not explicitly request.
I think that composure during emotionally charged situations is a form of service to the people involved, not a form of indifference.
I verify my understanding by restating instructions before carrying them out.
I feel that the moral pull of suffering is the experience that reveals to me most plainly what it means to be morally awake in a world full of need.
I tend to respond to emotional provocations with a measured internal response rather than a visceral one.
I process accounts of hardship primarily by considering what could be done differently rather than by dwelling on the pain itself.
I hold the view that my needs can always wait if someone else's are more pressing.
I find that I process conversations primarily through their informational content.
I am the person who pushes for a vote when discussion drags on.
I routinely turn down favors that would distract from my personal priorities.
I can work productively even in noisy, unpredictable environments.
I devote a fixed portion of my income to savings regardless of circumstances.
I sometimes need to physically move or pace to discharge the intensity of what I'm feeling.
When I have a vision, I wait to see if others share it before moving forward.
I find that the let-down after a period of excitement is as sharp as the excitement itself.
I tend to unilaterally escalate issues when I think a group is underreacting.
I prefer a general sense of direction over specific, rigid objectives.
I find that I miss when someone's phrasing of a request reveals how much power they think they have in the relationship.
I experience disappointment as a clear acknowledgment rather than a gut punch.
I find that the felt weight of another person's suffering is the consideration that most often tips my decision-making in a new direction.
I believe a project succeeds best with distributed leadership.
I believe that the practice of remaining objective during distressing events is a discipline that, far from diminishing empathic concern, channels it in more productive directions.
I feel that keeping score of what I've given defeats the purpose of giving.
I pick up on when a shared laugh is a bonding moment versus a nervous release.
I feel that hiring a personal trainer, coach, or therapist is an investment in my most important asset: myself.
I find that self-directed effort is the most energizing kind of work.
I pick up on the unspoken pecking order at a dinner party.
I feel that the weight of suffering in my moral outlook is the quality that has most consistently prevented me from accepting injustice as normal or inevitable.
I naturally speak first in group discussions.
I routinely spend my lunch break helping others with their tasks instead of resting.
I find it hard to tell when someone is being sarcastic versus sincere.
I often describe my emotional experiences using superlatives — best, worst, most intense.
I negotiate and make requests without scripted fallbacks, adapting to the flow of conversation.
I am careful to manage my energy across the day so I don't burn out before evening.
I tend to feel settled even when circumstances around me are in flux.
I tend to let multiple people contribute to important documents without one person holding the pen.
